t: Somatic complaints in anticipation of separation.
Not better explained by another mental disorder.
DSM-5-TR — duration ≥6 months in adults; ≥4 weeks in children.
ICD-11 — parallel; no separate age qualifier.
Clinical interview (also with parent in children).
Standardized interview (K-SADS for children, SCID-5 for adults).
Avoidance of situations with difficult escape.
Loss of speech in a specific context.
SAAS, K-SADS, MASC (Multidimensional Anxiety Scale for Children).
CBT (Coping Cat, FRIENDS programs for children; standard CBT for adults) — exposure-based, parental involvement critical in children. First-line.
USSR — moderate to severe cases or in cases of CBT failure; sertraline, fluoxetine (in children).
School return programs (school refusal) — graded exposure.
Family involvement — parental psychoeducation, avoidance of frequent phone calls/visits (accommodation strengthens symptoms).
AACAP Practice Parameter (Connolly S.D., Bernstein G.A. 2007) — CBT and SSRIs effective in children.
NICE — general guidelines for childhood anxiety (CBT first-line).
Walkup J.T. et al. NEJM 2008 (CAMS — Child/Adolescent Anxiety Multimodal Study) — CBT + sertraline combination superior to monotherapy.
Manualized CBT for children aged 7–17 years – 16 sessions, psychoeducation + relaxation + exposure + reward system (FEAR plan).
In Australia, developed group CBT for child and adolescent anxiety disorders.
— Structured exposure — child gradually returns to school environment; coordination of parent and school staff.
SPACE (Supportive Parenting for Anxious Childhood Emotions—Lebowitz E.) program—modifying parental accommodation of child's anxiety.
Remission in most children with early CBT.
Without treatment, continuation in adulthood leads to risk of other anxiety disorders and depression.
Myth 1: "Separation anxiety occurs only in childhood"
DSM-5 — separation anxiety disorder also diagnosed in adults — prevalence ~1.9%.
Myth 2: "The parent must give in to every demand to 'calm' the child"
Accommodation symptoms intensify; SPACE program or parent training is recommended.
Myth 3: "Separation anxiety is a result of poor parenting"
heritability ~40%; attachment and environment modulator. "Bad upbringing" explanation stigmatizing and incorrect.
Myth 4: "Leaving a child alone to 'get used to it' — this harsh approach cures them"
"Flooding" or uncontrolled exposure can be traumatic; graded, structured exposure recommended.
Myth 5: "Recovery is only possible with medication"
CBT first-line; SSRIs for moderate-severe cases or in combination.
Myth 6: "School refusal is a personality weakness and requires discipline"
School avoidance is a clinical anxiety symptom; punishment-based approach exacerbates the disorder.
Connolly S.D., Bernstein G.A. AACAP Practice Parameter for the Assessment and Treatment of Children and Adolescents With Anxiety Disorders. J Am Acad Child Adolesc Psychiatry 2007;46(2):267–283.
Walkup J.T. et al. CAMS: Cognitive behavioral therapy, sertraline, or a combination in childhood anxiety. NEJM 2008;359(26):2753–2766.
Kendall P.C. Treating anxiety disorders in children: results of a randomized clinical trial. J Consult Clin Psychol 1994;62(1):100–110.

Lebowitz E.R. et al. Parent-Based Treatment as Efficacious as Cognitive-Behavioral Therapy for Childhood Anxiety: A Randomized Noninferiority Study of Supportive Parenting for Anxious Childhood Emotions. J Am Acad Child Adolesc Psychiatry 2020;59(3):362–372.
